### Residential Metal Roofing

The Handy Roofer offers a full suite of residential metal roofing services across Greater Brisbane, including the Redlands, Brisbane, Logan, and Ipswich areas.

Specialising in high-quality metal roof replacements, we understand the importance of a durable roof to protect your home. In addition to roof replacements, The Handy Roofer also provides metal roof repairs, wall cladding, and fascia and gutter services.​​​

[Read More](https://www.thehandyroofer.com.au/residentialmetalroofing)

### Commercial Metal Roofing

The Handy Roofer is experienced in delivering on large and small commercial metal roofing projects. Working across Greater Brisbane, including Logan, Brisbane, Redlands and Ipswich.

The Handy Roofer has worked with commercial businesses, schools, churches and builders to deliver metal roof replacements, roof repairs, wall cladding, fascia and gutters.

\

    Metal Roof Replacement Vs. Metal Roof Restoration

    • Mark Baehnisch
    • Apr 21, 2023
    • 2 min read

    Updated: Oct 23, 2024

    Which One Is Right For Me?

    Roof replacement and roof restoration are two different approaches to addressing issues with a roof. Roof replacement involves removing the existing roof, upgrading any tie down requirements, and installing a completely new roof. Roof restoration involves repairing and refurbishing the existing roof with the intention of extending its lifespan, or sometimes simply applying a new coat of paint.

    Metal Roof Replacement

    Metal roof replacement is typically necessary when a roof is severely damaged, aged, or has reached the end of its lifespan. A complete roof replacement involves removing the existing roofing materials, inspecting the underlying structure for damage, and then installing new roofing material. Whilst this can be a costly and time-consuming process, it provides a fresh start for the entire roofing system, and in most cases it is backed up by a long term warranty from the installer. When installed to manufacturers specifications, a new BlueScope Steel roof can have a life expectancy of up to 70 years.

    Metal Roof Restoration

    Roof restoration, on the other hand, is a less expensive alternative to roof replacement. It involves repairing and refurbishing the existing roof to extend its lifespan. This can include cleaning the roof, fixing leaks, replacing damaged sheets and sealant, and applying protective coatings to the surface of the roof. Roof restorations generally come with no warranty against repairs, and a warranty on the paint coating.


    Making The Decision

    The decision between roof replacement and roof restoration depends on several factors, such as:


    · the age and condition of the roof,

    · the extent of damage or wear,

    · the overall cost of each option.


    If you’re unsure which course of action is best for your roof, contact us for a free inspection and quote.

    Metal Roofing: Roofing Jargon Decoded

    • megangunsser
    • Nov 24, 2023
    • 3 min read

    Updated: Oct 23, 2024

    Roofing plays a vital role in every Queensland home, providing protection against the elements. To make informed decisions about your roof, it's crucial to understand roofing terminology. To help, we'll unravel the common roofing lingo.


    Colorbond: A popular roofing material. Colorbond is a pre-painted steel product known for durability and versatility. It comes in various colours to suit your home's aesthetics.




    Tin Roof: While not always made of tin, this term is used colloquially to refer to metal roofing, like Colorbond or Zincalume.

    Gable Roof: A pitched roof with two slopes meeting at a ridge. It's a common design for homes, allowing for better ventilation.


    Hip Roof: A roof with slopes on all sides, with each side meeting at a ridge. Hip roofs are known for stability and resistance to strong winds, making them suitable for QLD's climate.


    Pitch: The steepness of a roof. Higher pitches are more common in regions with heavy rainfall, to encourage water runoff.


    Profile: The specific design and shape of a roofing material, impacting aesthetics, functionality, and water-shedding abilities.

    Eaves: The part of the roof that overhangs the walls. Eaves help protect your home's exterior from rain and sun.


    Fascia: The board running along the eaves. It holds the gutter in place and adds a finishing touch to your roof.


    Valley: The angle formed where two sloping roof sections meet. Valleys are critical for channelling rainwater away.


    Ridge: The highest point where two roof slopes meet. Ridge capping covers this area to prevent water from entering.


    Transition Flashing: A waterproof barrier used where different roof materials, slopes, or roof sections meet to prevent leaks.


    Dektite: A type of flashing designed to create a waterproof seal around roof penetrations, such as pipes or vents.

    Flashings: Metal pieces used to direct water away from vulnerable areas on the roof preventing leaks and protecting the structure. Vulnerable areas include chimneys, vents, air conditioners and valleys.


    Skylight: A roof window that allows natural light into a space.


    Whirly Bird: A rooftop ventilator that uses wind to improve ventilation and regulate temperature within the roof space.


    Gutter: A channel along the eaves that collects and directs rainwater away from your home. Regular cleaning is essential to prevent blockages.


    Box Gutter: A wide, shallow gutter that is designed to handle large volumes of water. Common in Queensland due to heavy rainfall.


    Sump: A roof drainage sump is a low point or depression on a flat or low-slope roof where water collects. Sumps are designed to help facilitate proper drainage by collecting rainwater and directing it towards the roof drains or downspouts.


    Downpipe: A pipe that carries water from the gutter to the ground or a drainage system.


    Solar Panels: Common in QLD due to the sunny climate, solar panels are installed on the roof to generate electricity from sunlight.


    Roof Truss: The structural framework that supports the roof. Trusses are designed for strength and stability.


    Batten: Thin strips of wood or metal used to support and secure roofing materials, creating a stable surface for installation.


    Tie-Downs: Essential in QLD to secure the roof to the structure, protecting against cyclones and high winds.


    Sarking: A reflective foil layer placed under the roof covering to enhance energy efficiency and reduce heat transfer.


    Insulation: Material that regulates temperature and conserves energy by preventing heat loss or gain in your home, promoting comfort and efficiency.


    Scribing: The process of fitting a piece of roofing or flashing material to an irregular or non- standard surface to ensure a proper fit.


    It’s helpful to understand roofing lingo when you need to repair or replace your roof. If your roofing contractor is using lingo you don’t understand, make sure you ask them for clarification. Knowing these terms will help you make informed decisions about your home's most important protection.

# Metal Roofing: Roofing Jargon Decoded

- megangunsser
- Nov 24, 2023
- 3 min read

Updated: Oct 23, 2024

Roofing plays a vital role in every Queensland home, providing protection against the elements. To make informed decisions about your roof, it's crucial to understand roofing terminology. To help, we'll unravel the common roofing lingo.

**Colorbond:** A popular roofing material. Colorbond is a pre-painted steel product known for durability and versatility. It comes in various colours to suit your home's aesthetics.

![Colorbond](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/80655c_39e2cb66223f4b8c982ed1fbb90d61ac~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_228,h_231,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,enc_avif,quality_auto/80655c_39e2cb66223f4b8c982ed1fbb90d61ac~mv2.png)

**Tin Roof:** While not always made of tin, this term is used colloquially to refer to metal roofing, like Colorbond or Zincalume.

![Metal Roofing](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/80655c_4b0236160ff449df9de661b397058c2d~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_350,h_199,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,enc_avif,quality_auto/80655c_4b0236160ff449df9de661b397058c2d~mv2.png)

**Gable Roof:** A pitched roof with two slopes meeting at a ridge. It's a common design for homes, allowing for better ventilation.

**Hip Roof:** A roof with slopes on all sides, with each side meeting at a ridge. Hip roofs are known for stability and resistance to strong winds, making them suitable for QLD's climate.

**Pitch:** The steepness of a roof. Higher pitches are more common in regions with heavy rainfall, to encourage water runoff.

**Profile:** The specific design and shape of a roofing material, impacting aesthetics, functionality, and water-shedding abilities.

![Metal Roof Profiles](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/80655c_65652d1f8ffa4294ba252aaf7f0d2662~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_291,h_322,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,enc_avif,quality_auto/80655c_65652d1f8ffa4294ba252aaf7f0d2662~mv2.png)

**Eaves:** The part of the roof that overhangs the walls. Eaves help protect your home's exterior from rain and sun.

**Fascia:** The board running along the eaves. It holds the gutter in place and adds a finishing touch to your roof.

![Metal Roofing](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/80655c_1876caca6a854d9885c9e3b70eb86fbd~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_740,h_210,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,enc_avif,quality_auto/80655c_1876caca6a854d9885c9e3b70eb86fbd~mv2.png)

**Valley:** The angle formed where two sloping roof sections meet. Valleys are critical for channelling rainwater away.

**Ridge:** The highest point where two roof slopes meet. Ridge capping covers this area to prevent water from entering.

**Transition Flashing:** A waterproof barrier used where different roof materials, slopes, or roof sections meet to prevent leaks.

![Metal Roof Flashings](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/80655c_3a190ac03a2b4d1894e78bbfb00ce5ca~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_247,h_171,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,enc_avif,quality_auto/80655c_3a190ac03a2b4d1894e78bbfb00ce5ca~mv2.png)

**Dektite:** A type of flashing designed to create a waterproof seal around roof penetrations, such as pipes or vents.

![Metal Roof Dektites](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/80655c_05cd2e8f12fd4044bdfbd12563e2452f~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_77,h_80,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/80655c_05cd2e8f12fd4044bdfbd12563e2452f~mv2.png)

**Flashings:** Metal pieces used to direct water away from vulnerable areas on the roof preventing leaks and protecting the structure. Vulnerable areas include chimneys, vents, air conditioners and valleys.

**Skylight:** A roof window that allows natural light into a space.

![Metal Roofing Skylights](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/80655c_3d257273ef144325b05d752b964e1570~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_117,h_97,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/80655c_3d257273ef144325b05d752b964e1570~mv2.png)

**Whirly Bird:** A rooftop ventilator that uses wind to improve ventilation and regulate temperature within the roof space.

![Metal Roofing Whirly Birds](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/80655c_35bee9b2a85d4187830bf8f420e80f0b~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_60,h_67,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/80655c_35bee9b2a85d4187830bf8f420e80f0b~mv2.png)

**Gutter:** A channel along the eaves that collects and directs rainwater away from your home. Regular cleaning is essential to prevent blockages.

**Box Gutter:** A wide, shallow gutter that is designed to handle large volumes of water. Common in Queensland due to heavy rainfall.

**Sump:** A roof drainage sump is a low point or depression on a flat or low-slope roof where water collects. Sumps are designed to help facilitate proper drainage by collecting rainwater and directing it towards the roof drains or downspouts.

**Downpipe:** A pipe that carries water from the gutter to the ground or a drainage system.

**Solar Panels:** Common in QLD due to the sunny climate, solar panels are installed on the roof to generate electricity from sunlight.

**Roof Truss:** The structural framework that supports the roof. Trusses are designed for strength and stability.

**Batten:** Thin strips of wood or metal used to support and secure roofing materials, creating a stable surface for installation.

**Tie-Downs:** Essential in QLD to secure the roof to the structure, protecting against cyclones and high winds.

**Sarking:** A reflective foil layer placed under the roof covering to enhance energy efficiency and reduce heat transfer.

**Insulation:** Material that regulates temperature and conserves energy by preventing heat loss or gain in your home, promoting comfort and efficiency.

**Scribing:** The process of fitting a piece of roofing or flashing material to an irregular or non- standard surface to ensure a proper fit.

![Metal Roofing](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/80655c_d87fe1db90b5495fb6d1307cc771caf4~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_56,h_38,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,blur_2,enc_avif,quality_auto/80655c_d87fe1db90b5495fb6d1307cc771caf4~mv2.png)

It’s helpful to understand roofing lingo when you need to repair or replace your roof. If your roofing contractor is using lingo you don’t understand, make sure you ask them for clarification. Knowing these terms will help you make informed decisions about your home's most important protection.

    Revitalizing Your Roof: The Transformation from Decramastic to Colorbond Steel

    • Mark Baehnisch
    • Aug 24, 2023
    • 3 min read

    Updated: Oct 23, 2024

    If you currently have a decramastic roof you might be considering upgrading to a Colorbond Steel roof. In this blog post, we will explore the benefits and process of converting decramastic roofs to Colorbond Steel, enabling you to make an informed decision about this significant home improvement project.



    Decramastic roofs, also known as pressed metal tile roofs, were popular in the mid-20th century due to their perceived durability and affordability. They typically consist of a base metal sheet with a bitumen coating and stone chip overlay. However, decramastic are known to be extremely fragile and susceptible to foot traffic and environmental damage. Maintenance to an existing decramastic roof is near impossible without denting the tiles. Dents in the tiles can lead to major leaking issues and a decline in the roof's visual appeal.


    Colorbond Steel, on the other hand, is a high-quality roofing material that offers numerous advantages over traditional decramastic roofs:


    Durability: Colorbond Steel is renowned for its exceptional durability, specifically designed to be able to withstand harsh Australian weather conditions, including heavy rain, strong winds, and hail. BlueScope Steel backs up their material with a warranty of up to 36 Years.


    Aesthetic Appeal: By converting to Colorbond Steel, you can give your home an instant facelift. The wide range of attractive colours and finishes available allows you to select a style that complements your home's architecture and enhances its overall appearance.


    Energy Efficiency: Colorbond Steel roofing is designed to reflect a significant amount of the sun's heat, reducing the need for excessive cooling during hot summer months. This can lead to energy savings and a more comfortable living environment. Colorbond Steel is also compatible with insulation building blanket products, providing further energy efficient options.


    Low Maintenance: Compared to decramastic roofs, Colorbond Steel requires minimal maintenance. Regular inspections and basic cleaning are usually sufficient to keep it in excellent condition for many years. Thanks to the durability of Colorbond Steel, it is capable of sustaining regular foot traffic when installed correctly.


    Environmental Sustainability: Colorbond Steel is made from recycled materials and is fully recyclable at the end of its lifespan. By choosing Colorbond Steel, you contribute to sustainable building practices and reduce the environmental impact of your home.


    The process of converting your existing decramastic roof to Colorbond Steel is slightly more complex than a standard metal to metal roof replacement, with additional steps being required to ensure the new roof installation complies with current codes and standards:


    1. Asbestos Testing: Some decramastic roof tiles contain asbestos within the bitumen coating used to adhere gritty finishes to the tiles and should be tested for asbestos contamination before works commence. Decramastic roof tiles without a gritty finish do not contain asbestos.


    2. Tie Downs: To comply with the National Construction requirements, some alterations need to be made to the existing roof framework. Existing decramastic roof tile battens are too small to support a Colorbond Steel roof, so new 40mm BlueScope Steel battens are installed to the existing frame at appropriate spacings to suit the chosen roof profile. Additional tie down upgrades may also be required, which is decided upon by a contracted Structural Engineer during the removal process.


    Converting your decramastic roof to Colorbond Steel offers a range of benefits, including enhanced durability, improved aesthetics, energy efficiency, and reduced maintenance requirements. While the conversion process may involve some investment, the long-term advantages and increased value it brings to your home make it a worthwhile upgrade.


    Feel free to contact the team at The Handy Roofer to arrange a free inspection and quote to assess your specific situation and embark on this exciting home improvement journey.

    Preserving History: Our Expertise in Heritage Roof Replacements

    • Mark Baehnisch
    • Sep 6, 2023
    • 3 min read

    Updated: Oct 23, 2024

    Roof replacement is a significant undertaking for any homeowner, but when it comes to heritage properties, the stakes are even higher. Heritage homes are not just dwellings; they are pieces of history, and preserving their architectural integrity is a responsibility we take seriously. In this blog, we will delve into how we specialise in heritage roof replacements and the unique challenges and considerations involved in such projects.






    Understanding Heritage Roofing

    Before we delve into the specifics of heritage roof replacements, let's understand what makes a roof 'heritage'. Heritage roofs are typically found on buildings of historical or cultural significance, such as period homes, churches, government buildings, and more. These roofs often feature traditional materials, unique designs, and craftsmanship that reflect the era in which they were constructed.


    Challenges of Heritage Roof Replacements

    Replacing a heritage roof requires an understanding of historical roofing materials, techniques, and a commitment to maintaining authenticity. Here are some challenges commonly faced in heritage roof replacements:


    1. Material Authenticity: Finding the right materials that match the originals can be a challenge. Heritage roofs often use materials which may not be readily available or manufactured in the same way today. We work together with a network of suppliers who specialise in sourcing heritage materials to ensure we can overcome this hurdle with ease.


    2. Preservation of Historical Value: Heritage properties often have strict preservation guidelines. Any changes made to the roof must respect the historical value of the building. This includes maintaining the original design and appearance.


    3. Structural Integrity: Older buildings may have structural issues that need to be addressed during the roof replacement. This requires a thorough assessment by suitably qualified personnel to determine if structural upgrades are required.


    Our Expertise in Heritage Roof Replacements

    At The Handy Roofer we have a broad range of experience with heritage roof replacement projects covering everything from planning to completion. Jeremy and our team have worked on a number of local iconic heritage buildings, including Hanworth House, St Stephens Cathedral Brisbane, and Milton House, to name a few. Below is a brief overview of how we handle each project:


    1. Historical Research: We begin every heritage roof replacement project with extensive historical research. This involves studying old photographs, architectural plans, and records to understand the original roof's design and materials.


    2. Material Sourcing: We work diligently to source the most authentic roofing materials available. In cases where exact matches are not possible, we collaborate with skilled fabricators to custom-create materials and fittings that replicate the originals.


    3. Preservation Techniques: Our team is well-versed in preservation techniques that ensure the heritage value of the property is maintained. This includes replicating traditional craftsmanship and restoration methods.


    4. Structural Assessment: We conduct a thorough structural assessment to identify and address any underlying issues. This ensures that the new roof will not only look authentic but also function effectively.


    5. Compliance and Approvals: We navigate the complex world of permits and approvals, working closely with heritage authorities and local councils to ensure that the roof replacement meets all regulatory and preservation standards.


    Preserving the charm and historical significance of heritage properties is both an art and a science. Our expertise in heritage roof replacements is driven by a deep respect for history and a commitment to craftsmanship. When you entrust us with the task of replacing a heritage roof, you are not just investing in a new roof; you are investing in the preservation of a cultural legacy. With our dedication to authenticity and attention to detail, we ensure that the roof over your heritage property stands the test of time, just as the history it represents.

    QBCC Home Warranty Insurance Explained

    • megangunsser
    • Nov 17, 2023
    • 2 min read

    Building or renovating a home is an exciting and significant undertaking, but it also comes with responsibilities and risks. In Queensland, the Queensland Building and Construction Commission (QBCC) Home Warranty Insurance plays a crucial role in protecting both homeowners and tradespeople. This article sheds light on why QBCC Home Warranty Insurance is required and the benefits it offers to all stakeholders involved in the construction industry.


    Why it’s Required?

    Consumer Protection: One of the primary reasons for requiring QBCC Home Warranty Insurance is to protect consumers – the homeowners. It safeguards their interests in case the builder does not fulfill their contractual obligations or becomes insolvent.


    Mandatory for Certain Work: QBCC Home Warranty Insurance is mandated under the Queensland Building and Construction Commission Act 1991 (QBCC Act). The insurance is mandatory for certain residential construction work that exceeds $3,300, including new homes, renovations, and extensions. It provides assurance that the builder is financially accountable for their work. Failure to have Home Warranty Insurance can result in legal penalties.


    Benefits of QBCC Home Warranty Insurance

    For Homeowners:

    Peace of Mind: Homeowners can have peace of mind knowing that their investment is protected. If the builder fails to complete the work or it is defective, they have a financial safety net.

    Conflict Resolution: In the event of a dispute, QBCC Home Warranty Insurance can help resolve issues without lengthy and costly legal proceedings. It provides an avenue for homeowners to seek compensation for subpar work.


    For Tradespeople:

    Compliance: As mentioned previously, it’s mandatory to have QBCC Home Warranty Insurance for certain residential work. This encourages a more professional and accountable construction industry.

    Financial Protection: Tradespeople are protected from the financial repercussions of potential disputes or insolvency. This security allows them to focus on delivering quality work.


    For the Industry:

    Enhanced Reputation: The presence of QBCC Home Warranty Insurance within the industry fosters trust among stakeholders, contributing to a positive industry reputation.

    Reduced Disputes: The insurance helps minimise disputes between homeowners and tradespeople. A more streamlined dispute resolution process benefits everyone involved.

    Consumer Confidence: When consumers have faith in the system and the protections it provides, it fosters confidence in the residential construction industry. This can lead to increased demand for construction services.


    QBCC Home Warranty Insurance is a crucial safeguard for homeowners and tradespeople in Queensland. It ensures that residential construction work is completed to a satisfactory standard and provides financial protection in cases of non-compliance or insolvency. For homeowners, it offers peace of mind, while the industry at large enjoys a boost in reputation and consumer confidence.


    More information on QBCC Home Warranty Insurance can be found at https://www.qbcc.qld.gov.au/running-business/home-warranty-obligations.

It provides assurance that the builder is financially accountable for their work. Failure to have Home Warranty Insurance can result in legal penalties.

**Benefits of QBCC Home Warranty Insurance**

**For Homeowners:**

**Peace of Mind:** Homeowners can have peace of mind knowing that their investment is protected. If the builder fails to complete the work or it is defective, they have a financial safety net.

**Conflict Resolution:** In the event of a dispute, QBCC Home Warranty Insurance can help resolve issues without lengthy and costly legal proceedings. It provides an avenue for homeowners to seek compensation for subpar work.

**For Tradespeople:**

**Compliance:** As mentioned previously, it’s mandatory to have QBCC Home Warranty Insurance for certain residential work. This encourages a more professional and accountable construction industry.

**Financial Protection:** Tradespeople are protected from the financial repercussions of potential disputes or insolvency. This security allows them to focus on delivering quality work.

**For the Industry:**

**Enhanced Reputation:** The presence of QBCC Home Warranty Insurance within the industry fosters trust among stakeholders, contributing to a positive industry reputation.

**Reduced Disputes:** The insurance helps minimise disputes between homeowners and tradespeople. A more streamlined dispute resolution process benefits everyone involved.

**Consumer Confidence:** When consumers have faith in the system and the protections it provides, it fosters confidence in the residential construction industry. This can lead to increased demand for construction services.

QBCC Home Warranty Insurance is a crucial safeguard for homeowners and tradespeople in Queensland. It ensures that residential construction work is completed to a satisfactory standard and provides financial protection in cases of non-compliance or insolvency. For homeowners, it offers peace of mind, while the industry at large enjoys a boost in reputation and consumer confidence.

More information on QBCC Home Warranty Insurance can be found at [https://www.qbcc.qld.gov.au/running-business/home-warranty-obligations](https://www.qbcc.qld.gov.au/running-business/home-warranty-obligations).

    Elevating Your Roof: Replacing Concrete Tiles with Colorbond Metal

    • megangunsser
    • Nov 7, 2023
    • 3 min read

    Updated: Oct 23, 2024

    To protect your home from the elements, a robust and reliable roofing system is essential. One popular choice for roofing material in QLD is Colorbond metal roofing. This blog will guide you through the process of converting concrete tile roofs to Colorbond metal roofs.



    Why Convert to Colorbond Metal Roofing?

    Colorbond roofing has gained immense popularity for several reasons:


    1. Durability: Colorbond metal roofs are designed to withstand the harsh Australian climate, including intense heat, UV radiation, and severe storms. They are highly resistant to cracking, chipping, and peeling.

    2. Energy Efficiency: The reflective properties of Colorbond roofing can help reduce your cooling costs during hot Queensland summers.

    3. Low Maintenance: Colorbond roofs are low-maintenance and easy to clean, saving you time and money over the long term.

    4. Style Variety: Colorbond offers a wide range of colour and style options, allowing homeowners to choose a roof that compliments their home’s aesthetic.

    5. Longevity: Colorbond roofs have a long lifespan, making them a cost-effective investment.


    Now, let’s dive into the steps and considerations for replacing concrete tiles with Colorbond metal.


    Step 1: Inspection and Consultation

    Initiate the conversion process by engaging a professional roofing contractor experienced in roof conversions. They will conduct an inspection of your existing concrete tile roof, assessing its condition and identifying any structural issues. This ensures a smoother conversion.


    Step 2: Obtain Necessary Permits

    In Queensland, building permits and approvals are required for any significant alterations to your home, including roof conversions. Queensland Building and Construction Commission (QBCC) Home Warranty Insurance is also required for residential roof work over $3300. Your chosen contractor should be well-versed in local regulations and will assist you in obtaining the requisite permits and insurance.


    Step 3: Silica Dust Considerations

    When removing the existing concrete tiles, it’s essential to be aware of the potential release of silica dust, which can be harmful to health. Adequate safety measures, such as water suppression and proper disposal of materials should be implemented in accordance with Queensland’s Workplace Health and Safety regulations.


    Step 4: Concrete Tile Removal

    The next step is removing the existing concrete tiles, taking care to prevent any damage to the underlying structure. In many cases, these tiles can be recycled or disposed of in an environmentally responsible manner.


    Step 5: Roof Framework Evaluation

    After the tile removal, the roof framework should be inspected for any damage or necessary repairs. In some cases, reinforcement is required to support the expected wind load of the new Colorbond roof, as metal roofs are approximately 10 times lighter than concrete tile roofs.


    Step 6: Roof Insulation

    Queensland’s climate demands proper insulation to maintain a comfortable indoor temperature. Consider adding insulation material between the roof frame and the Colorbond sheets to enhance energy efficiency and reduce heat transfer.


    Step 7: Tie-Down Upgrades

    Given Queensland’s propensity for extreme weather events, it’s crucial to evaluate and, if necessary, upgrade the tie-down system for your new Colorbond roof. Ensuring it can withstand strong winds and stormy conditions is vital.


    Step 8: Installation of Colorbond Roofing

    Your roofing contractor will proceed with the installation of Colorbond metal sheets following the manufacturer’s guidelines and the National Construction Code.


    Step 9: Safety and Quality Assurance

    Before the project is considered complete, your contractor should conduct an inspection to ensure the roof meets all safety and quality standards. This includes checking for loose screws, ensuring proper sealing, and verifying that the roof is securely anchored.


    Converting a concrete tile roof to a Colorbond metal roof is a wise investment that offers benefits including durability, energy efficiency, and aesthetic appeal. However, it is essential to work with experienced professionals who understand the specific requirements and regulations of tile conversions. Feel free to contact us with any questions and we can complete a free inspection and quote.

    Colorbond Vs Zincalume Roofing and Rainwater Products

    • Mark Baehnisch
    • Apr 28, 2023
    • 2 min read

    Updated: Oct 23, 2024


    Colorbond and Zincalume are two types of steel products commonly used for roofing and cladding throughout Australia. Both are manufactured by BlueScope Steel, an Australian steel company. BlueScope Steel is well-known for its ability to manufacture steel products that are durable, strong, and corrosion-resistant, suitable for the Australian climate.


    Colorbond Steel is a pre-painted steel product that is available in a wide range of colours. It’s made by applying a zinc and aluminium alloy coating to a steel substrate, followed by a primer coat and a topcoat of paint. The paint used on Colorbond Steel is specially formulated to withstand harsh Australian weather conditions, including UV radiation, extreme heat, and heavy rainfall. Colorbond Steel is a popular choice for roofing, wall cladding, and rainwater goods due to its aesthetic appeal, versatility, and long lifespan. To see the current Colorbond range, visit https://colorbond.com/.



    On the other hand, Zincalume Steel is an unpainted steel product that has a distinctive silver-grey appearance, like old, galvanised roof sheeting that many Australians are familiar with. Zincalume Steel is made by coating the steel substrate with a layer of zinc and aluminium alloy, which provides excellent corrosion resistance. Unlike Colorbond Steel, Zincalume Steel doesn’t have a painted finish and is often used for roofing and cladding in industrial and commercial buildings. It is also commonly used for rainwater goods, fencing, and structural applications.


    In summary, with regards to Colorbond vs Zincalume roofing:

    • Both Colorbond and Zincalume are high quality steel products manufactured by BlueScope Steel.

    • They have slightly different features and applications.

    • Colorbond is slightly more expensive than Zincalume.

    • Colorbond is often used in highly visible applications.

    • Both products are backed by BlueScope Steel’s warranty for up to 36 years, depending on the location of your project. For more information on the Colorbond and Zincalume material warranties, visit https://warranties.bluescopesteel.com.au/site/.

    Feel free to contact The Handy Roofer team to arrange an obligation free quote. This includes a free simulation of your own property to visualise how your finished project will look in either Zincalume or any one of the available Colorbond colours.

# Colorbond Vs Zincalume Roofing and Rainwater Products

- Mark Baehnisch
- Apr 28, 2023
- 2 min read

Updated: Oct 23, 2024

![Colorbond metal roofing](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/80655c_4132a3cf7f7c4f8dab10aedf999b2a93~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_240,h_202,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,enc_avif,quality_auto/80655c_4132a3cf7f7c4f8dab10aedf999b2a93~mv2.png)

Colorbond and Zincalume are two types of steel products commonly used for roofing and cladding throughout Australia. Both are manufactured by BlueScope Steel, an Australian steel company. BlueScope Steel is well-known for its ability to manufacture steel products that are durable, strong, and corrosion-resistant, suitable for the Australian climate.

Colorbond Steel is a pre-painted steel product that is available in a wide range of colours. It’s made by applying a zinc and aluminium alloy coating to a steel substrate, followed by a primer coat and a topcoat of paint. The paint used on Colorbond Steel is specially formulated to withstand harsh Australian weather conditions, including UV radiation, extreme heat, and heavy rainfall. Colorbond Steel is a popular choice for roofing, wall cladding, and rainwater goods due to its aesthetic appeal, versatility, and long lifespan. To see the current Colorbond range, visit [https://colorbond.com/](https://colorbond.com/).

![Colorbond metal roofing](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/80655c_39e2cb66223f4b8c982ed1fbb90d61ac~mv2.png/v1/fill/w_207,h_210,al_c,q_85,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,enc_avif,quality_auto/80655c_39e2cb66223f4b8c982ed1fbb90d61ac~mv2.png)

![Colorbond metal roofing](https://static.wixstatic.com/media/80655c_341877b71c024fb3b45321d5728f42ce~mv2.jpg/v1/fill/w_740,h_243,al_c,q_80,usm_0.66_1.00_0.01,enc_avif,quality_auto/80655c_341877b71c024fb3b45321d5728f42ce~mv2.jpg)

On the other hand, Zincalume Steel is an unpainted steel product that has a distinctive silver-grey appearance, like old, galvanised roof sheeting that many Australians are familiar with. Zincalume Steel is made by coating the steel substrate with a layer of zinc and aluminium alloy, which provides excellent corrosion resistance. Unlike Colorbond Steel, Zincalume Steel doesn’t have a painted finish and is often used for roofing and cladding in industrial and commercial buildings. It is also commonly used for rainwater goods, fencing, and structural applications.

In summary, with regards to Colorbond vs Zincalume roofing:

- Both Colorbond and Zincalume are high quality steel products manufactured by BlueScope Steel.

- They have slightly different features and applications.

- Colorbond is slightly more expensive than Zincalume.

- Colorbond is often used in highly visible applications.

- Both products are backed by BlueScope Steel’s warranty for up to 36 years, depending on the location of your project. For more information on the Colorbond and Zincalume material warranties, visit [https://warranties.bluescopesteel.com.au/site/](https://warranties.bluescopesteel.com.au/site/).


Feel free to contact The Handy Roofer team to arrange an obligation free quote. This includes a free simulation of your own property to visualise how your finished project will look in either Zincalume or any one of the available Colorbond colours.
